behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) specified in the description.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The parsing/extraction logic can be tested at Unit, but the real failure mode is the LLM generating a mismatched signature — wrong parameter names, wrong types, or wrong return type. A mocked LLM always returns what we tell it, hiding this class of failure entirely. LLM-DEP: Running this test 100 times with the real LLM could produce different signature shapes; correctness depends on actual model output quality, not just deterministic parsing. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed beyond the LLM API. MULTI-STEP: Single generation step. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior says 'generated function signature matches the expected interface' — this is a claim about LLM output quality, not about parsing logic alone. No text disqualifies Agentic in favor of Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real service with a natural language description that specifies a precise interface (e.g., 'a function named multiply_matrix(a: List[List[float]], b: List[List[float]]) -> List[List[float]]'). Extract the generated function's AST signature and assert parameter names, type annotations, and return type all match the specification exactly.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: 'First or a subsequent attempt' explicitly requires the full generate→test→refine→test loop in which multiple real LLM calls are made in sequence. LLM-DEP: Whether the implementation actually passes the test suite depends on real LLM output quality across potentially multiple rounds — a deterministic stub always passes, hiding failures. REAL-INFRA: Running generated code against test cases requires real subprocess or dynamic in-process code execution; a fully in-process fake of code execution would hide failures where generated code has subtle runtime bugs. REJECTION-GATE: 'First or a subsequent attempt' means we cannot collapse this to a single Agentic call; the failure mode is in the multi-step feedback loop, not just a single generation.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full service pipeline with the real LLM and a real test executor against a supplied test suite for a non-trivial function. Assert that by the final attempt the implementation passes every provided test case, and that the service reports success rather than exhausting all retries.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is a data-flow bug between the test runner output and the next prompt construction — this only appears when the two modules are wired together. A pure unit test of the prompt builder in isolation cannot catch a bug where the orchestrator fails to forward failure output to the builder. REAL-INFRA: A fake test runner that returns a canned failure string is sufficient; the behavior is about whether failure messages reach the next prompt, not about real subprocess behavior. MOCK-HIDE: Capturing the exact string passed to the mocked LLM client on the second call is sufficient to verify inclusion. LLM-DEP: No — we are checking prompt content, not LLM output quality; the assertion is deterministic. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network needed. (b) No real AI/ML output needed. (c) Mocked LLM calls, not real agentic steps.
behaviors.test_description: Wire together the orchestrator, a stub test runner configured to return specific failure messages on the first call and pass on the second, and a mock LLM client that captures every prompt it receives. Invoke the service and assert that the second LLM call's prompt contains the exact failure messages produced by the first test run.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The retry-count ceiling and structured-error construction span the orchestration loop and the response serializer. A unit test of either in isolation cannot catch a bug where the orchestrator runs 4 times or omits attempt #2 from the error envelope. Both modules must be wired together. REAL-INFRA: A stub test runner always returning failure is sufficient; no real subprocess or OS behavior is needed. LLM-DEP: The LLM can be mocked to return syntactically broken code every time — correctness of the stop-at-3 logic is deterministic. INTEGRATION-BRAKE: (a) No real OS/network. (b) No real AI/ML output. (c) Three mocked LLM calls, not real agentic steps. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior is about counting and serialization, not generation quality, so no justification to escalate beyond Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Configure the service with a mock LLM client that always returns syntactically broken code and a stub test runner that always reports failures. Invoke the service and assert: exactly 3 LLM calls were made, the return value is a structured error type, and the error payload contains all three attempts each paired with their respective test failure output.

behaviors.description: The full loop — generate, test, refine, test again — produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: MULTI-STEP: 'Generate, test, refine, test again' is explicitly a multi-step agentic pipeline; the behavior cannot be exercised with a single LLM call. LLM-DEP: 'A single LLM call typically gets wrong' directly asserts that the value is in the feedback loop improving real LLM output across attempts — a stub always returns what we program it to, hiding whether the real iterative refinement mechanism actually works. REAL-INFRA: Running generated code against real test cases requires real code execution; an in-process fake would mask runtime errors in generated implementations. REJECTION-GATE: The behavior explicitly calls out the multi-step loop and the fact that single-call generation fails — no text disqualifies Workflow in favor of Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Run the full service against the real LLM using a canonical function known to fail on the first attempt (e.g., one requiring careful handling of an edge case like empty input or integer overflow). Assert that at least one intermediate attempt fails its tests, that each subsequent prompt contains the prior failure messages, and that the final attempt produces a passing implementation.
